name: Hornos – tour around El Yelmo and Siles
name_original: Hornos – vuelta por El Yelmo y Siles
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Hornos are situated within the Sierra de Segura region, part of the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park in Spain. The area features a mountainous landscape with varied terrain, offering both challenging climbs and scenic descents. Road cyclists can explore routes that wind through natural parks and past features like the Embalse del Tranco de Beas and the Castillo de Segura de la Sierra. The region provides a diverse backdrop for road cycling activities. Best road cycling routes around Hornos The most popular road cycling route is Hornos – tour around El Yelmo and Siles, a 51.6 miles (83.0 km) trail that takes 4 hours 27 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers extensive views of the surrounding natural park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Embalse del Tranco de Beas and ascent to Mirador de Huelguecillas — circular from Hornos, a difficult 46.8 miles (75.3 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain and panoramic vistas over the reservoir and surrounding mountains. Local road cyclists also love the Ascent to the Castillo de Segura de la Sierra — circular by Orcera, a 23.2 miles (37.4 km) trail leading through varied terrain to a historic castle, often completed in about 1 hour 59 minutes. Road cycling around Hornos is defined by mountainous terrain, natural park landscapes, and reservoir views. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels. The routes in Hornos are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 8 reviews. More than 800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Hornos's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Hornos?
A: There are over 30 road cycling routes around Hornos, offering a diverse range of experiences within the Sierra de Segura region. These routes are primarily categorized as moderate to difficult, catering to cyclists looking for a challenge.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Hornos?
A: Road cycling around Hornos features a mountainous landscape with varied terrain. You can expect challenging climbs and exhilarating descents, winding through natural parks and offering panoramic views. The region is part of the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, providing a stunning natural backdrop.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly road cycling routes in Hornos?
A: While Hornos is known for its more challenging terrain, with 23 difficult and 10 moderate routes, there are no routes specifically classified as 'easy' for road cycling. Cyclists seeking family-friendly options might consider routes with less elevation gain, though they will still encounter varied mountain roads.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available around Hornos?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Hornos are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. For example, the route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345001169/embalse-del-tranco-de-beas-and-ascent-to-mirador-de-huelguecillas-circular-from-hornos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse del Tranco de Beas and ascent to Mirador de Huelguecillas — circular from Hornos&lt;/a&gt; offers significant elevation gain and panoramic vistas over the reservoir.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can see along the road cycling routes?
A: Road cycling routes around Hornos often pass by significant natural and historical landmarks. You can cycle past the &lt;strong&gt;Embalse del Tranco de Beas&lt;/strong&gt; reservoir or ascend towards the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1277897445/ascent-to-the-castillo-de-segura-de-la-sierra-circular-by-orcera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Segura de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt;. The region also offers views of prominent peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5298253" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Yelmo Summit&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the routes in Hornos?
A: The road cycling routes in Hornos are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 8 reviews. Cyclists often praise the extensive views of the natural park, the challenging climbs, and the panoramic vistas over the reservoir and surrounding mountains.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the Tranco Reservoir?
A: Absolutely. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345001169/embalse-del-tranco-de-beas-and-ascent-to-mirador-de-huelguecillas-circular-from-hornos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse del Tranco de Beas and ascent to Mirador de Huelguecillas — circular from Hornos&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345010776/ascent-to-el-yelmo-circular-route-around-the-tranco-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to El Yelmo — circular route around the Tranco Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; are specifically designed to showcase the stunning views of the Tranco Reservoir and its surroundings.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Hornos?
A: Given its mountainous location within the Sierra de Segura, the spring and autumn months generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling in Hornos, with milder temperatures. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring colder weather and potential for snow at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any routes that lead to specific mountain peaks or viewpoints?
A: Yes, several routes offer ascents towards or views of significant peaks. For instance, the route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009274084/hornos-tour-around-el-yelmo-and-siles" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hornos – tour around El Yelmo and Siles&lt;/a&gt; provides extensive views of the natural park, including the area around &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5298253" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Yelmo Summit&lt;/a&gt;. You can also find viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5446556" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Risca del Guijarrón&lt;/a&gt; in the vicinity.

Q: Where can I find places to rest or refuel along the routes?
A: While specific cafes are not detailed for every route, the region around Hornos, being part of a natural park, has some facilities. You may find shelters like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3980308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campo del Espino Shelter&lt;/a&gt; or camping areas such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6373433" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camping Llanos de Arance&lt;/a&gt;, which can serve as rest stops. It's advisable to plan your route and carry sufficient supplies.

Q: Are there any routes that explore the area around El Yelmo?
A: Yes, the area around El Yelmo is a popular destination for road cyclists. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009274084/hornos-tour-around-el-yelmo-and-siles" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hornos – tour around El Yelmo and Siles&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1276045980/el-yelmo-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Yelmo Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt; offer challenging rides with extensive views of this prominent peak and the surrounding natural park.
tips: Prepare to climb. The road begins to climb as soon as you leave Hornos. Beautiful beginning of the path angling between the trees. The final 5.4km stretch begins as you pass a stone ruin. The difference in altitude is 510m at an average of 6% until reaching El Yelmo at 1780m above sea level.
The town of Siles is located in the Guadalimar river valley, in the Sierra de la Segura. The town skyline is dominated by El Cubo, a medieval defensive structure left over from a 13th century castle.

A small town with narrow streets and buildings with whitewashed walls in the province of Jaén. The Castillo de Hornos is located in a strategic position at the highest point. Protect the trade routes in and out of the Sierra de Cazorla and Sierra de Segura mountains. The castle was built by Muslims in the 9th century and now functions as a museum.
